monsters.name: Alienoid
monsters.tagline: The Space Challenger. Turns every disadvantage into fuel.
monsters.content_html:
Overview

Nothing goes to waste. Damage converts to energy, the card market becomes a tool, and every bad situation becomes an opportunity.

As an Intermediate monster with a Harvester and Collector playstyle, Alienoid rewards patience and board reading over brute force.

Playstyle

The evolution cards define this clearly: Anger Batteries converts incoming damage directly into energy, Superior Alien Technology makes expensive cards affordable, and Adapting Technology reshapes the market on demand.

The more pressure opponents apply, the more fuel Alienoid generates. The core loop is: absorb hits, convert to energy, buy powerful cards, escalate.

Evolution Cards

Alienoid's eight evolution cards reward adaptation and energy management. You start with one in hand and draw more by rolling three or more hearts.

Superior Alien Technology (Permanent)

Buy KEEP cards at half cost (rounded up). When you do, place a UFO token on the card. At the start of your turn, roll a die for each KEEP card with a UFO token. Discard any card that rolls a CLAW. Maximum 3 UFO-tokened cards at a time.

Alienoid's most explosive card. Grabbing a high cost card for half price is potentially game-winning, but the discard risk is real. Prioritise cards that give immediate value rather than long-term engine pieces that need time to pay off.

Mothership Support (Permanent)

Once per turn, spend 1 ENERGY to gain 1 HEART.

Converts energy directly into survivability. Lets you stay in Tokyo longer than opponents expect and keeps your health buffer stable without relying on heart dice.

Exotic Arms (Permanent)

Before you roll, place 2 ENERGY on this card. If you roll at least 3 CLAWS, take the energy back and deal 2 extra damage to each monster you wound. If you don't, lose the 2 ENERGY and lose 2 HEART.

High risk, high reward. Only gamble with this when you have energy to spare. The downside is punishing.

Funny Looking But Dangerous (Permanent)

If you roll at least three 2s, every other monster loses 1 HEART.

Passive and consistent once active. Rolling three 2s happens more often than expected and the chip damage accumulates quickly across the table.

Adapting Technology (Permanent)

Comes with 3 ALIEN tokens. On your turn, remove a token to discard the 3 face-up Power cards and replace them with 3 new ones. Discarded when all tokens are used.

A controlled market reset. Best saved for when the market is dead or when an opponent is clearly eyeing a card you want to deny them.

Anger Batteries (Temporary)

Gain 1 ENERGY for each HEART you lost this turn.

Alienoid's most on-brand card. Converts punishment directly into fuel. Play it after a heavy damage turn to refill your engine entirely.

Precision Field Support (Temporary)

Draw Power cards until you reveal a KEEP card costing 4 ENERGY or less. Take it for free and discard the rest.

A free card from the deck. Best used when the face-up market has nothing worth buying.

Alien Scourge (Temporary)

Gain 2 STAR.

Hold it until you are 2 VP from winning and close out cleanly.

Tips & Strategies

Early Game

  • Prioritise lightning bolts over everything else. The entire kit runs on energy.
  • Your opening evolution sets the gameplan. Mothership Support means lean into sustain. Superior Alien Technology means pivot to card buying immediately.

Late Game

  • Hold Alien Scourge until you are exactly 2 VP from winning.
  • Exotic Arms is safer to gamble with once you have a large energy buffer built up.

Best Evolution Combos

  • Anger Batteries + Exotic Arms — take damage, refuel, then bet the energy on a big claw turn.
  • Superior Alien Technology + Precision Field Support — half-price buys and a free card in the same round.
  • Mothership Support + Funny Looking But Dangerous — heal passively while dealing chip damage every turn.

Common Mistakes

  • Gambling Exotic Arms when low on health or energy, the penalty hits both
  • Using all Adapting Technology tokens too early before opponents have stockpiled energy
  • Playing Anger Batteries before taking damage that turn, it needs to be reactive
  • Rolling claws when lightning bolts are almost always the better choice

monsters.name: Cyber Kitty
monsters.tagline: Nine lives, zero mercy. The cat always lands on her feet.
monsters.content_html:
Overview

Chaos incarnate. Fragile, unpredictable, and capable of swinging a game completely in a single turn. The evolutions punish opponents in Tokyo, steal resources directly, and culminate in one of the most dramatic comeback mechanics in the game.

Rated Advanced with a Disruptor and Smasher playstyle, Cyber Kitty rewards players who enjoy high-variance, high-reward gameplay and aren't afraid of risky turns.

Playstyle

Two things: rolling ones and stealing resources. Meow Missile and Mouse Hunter both trigger off a single 1 in your final roll, turning a normally wasted die face into a free claw and a free VP simultaneously. Play With Your Food converts strong claw turns into direct resource theft from Tokyo occupants.

Nine Lives means elimination isn't the end. The core loop is: roll ones to amplify, steal resources, hit Tokyo hard, trigger Nine Lives, win from nowhere.

Evolution Cards

Cyber Kitty's eight evolution cards build a cohesive kit around rolling ones, stealing resources, and surviving situations that would eliminate any other monster.

Meow Missile (Permanent)

If you roll at least one 1, add a CLAW to your roll.

A passive damage amplifier. Most players reroll ones away out of habit. With this active, a single 1 in your final roll means a free claw on top of everything else. Stack with Mouse Hunter for turns where keeping ones is the primary goal.

Mouse Hunter (Permanent)

If you roll at least one 1, gain 1 STAR.

A free VP every turn you end with a 1 in your roll. Combined with Meow Missile, a single 1 simultaneously gives a free claw and a free VP. Keeping one die showing a 1 becomes a legitimate strategy rather than a concession.

Feline Motor (Permanent)

During other monsters' movement phases, if Tokyo is empty, you can take control of it instead of the monster whose turn it is.

Tokyo control that costs nothing on your own turn. If anyone yields and Tokyo empties during their turn, slide in before the active monster gets there. Opponents can't account for it because it triggers on their turn, not yours.

Play With Your Food (Permanent)

When a monster in Tokyo must lose at least 2 HEART from your CLAWS, you can make them lose 2 HEART fewer and instead steal 1 STAR and 1 ENERGY from them directly.

Turns every strong claw turn into a choice. Deal full damage, or steal a star and energy from the Tokyo occupant? Against energy-rich monsters the theft is often worth more than the extra damage.

Cat Nip (Temporary)

Play at the start of your turn. You only have one roll this turn, but double the result.

The highest ceiling single-turn card in the kit. Six dice become effectively twelve. Play it on a turn where the first roll is already strong. A mediocre roll doubled is still mediocre.

Electro-Scratch (Temporary)

All other monsters lose 1 HEART.

A board-wide ping that hits everyone simultaneously. Use it to finish off a weakened monster, pressure the table, or soften up a Tokyo occupant before a claw turn.

Mega Purr (Temporary)

All other monsters give you 1 ENERGY or 1 STAR (they choose).

Mandatory tribute from the entire table. Every opponent must hand over either energy or a VP with no option to give nothing. Best played when opponents are energy-rich and star-rich so there is no easy choice.

Nine Lives (Temporary)

If you reach 0 HEART, discard all your cards including evolutions, lose all energy and stars, and leave Tokyo. Gain 9 HEART and 9 STAR, and continue playing.

The most dramatic evolution card in the game. Instead of being eliminated, reset to 9 health and 9 VP instantly. The cost is severe, every card and resource gone, but from 9 VP two good turns can win the game. Hold it as long as possible and let opponents think they can eliminate you.

Tips & Strategies

Early Game

  • Get Meow Missile and Mouse Hunter active together as fast as possible. A single 1 in your roll then generates a free claw and a free VP every turn.
  • Keep Nine Lives in hand from the moment you draw it. Its value comes entirely from being a surprise.

Late Game

  • Trigger Nine Lives at exactly 0 health, ideally when opponents are about to pile on. The instant 9 VP reset can flip the entire game.
  • Cat Nip is the best closer when health is stable. Doubling a strong natural roll can end the game in a single turn.

Best Evolution Combos

  • Meow Missile + Mouse Hunter + Cat Nip — double your dice with both one-triggers active for a turn that generates VP, claws, and raw dice value simultaneously.
  • Play With Your Food + Feline Motor — steal resources from Tokyo occupants with claws, then slide into Tokyo the moment they yield.
  • Nine Lives + Electro-Scratch or Mega Purr — after triggering Nine Lives at 9 VP, immediately play a resource grab or board ping to accelerate the closing push.

Common Mistakes

  • Rerolling ones away out of habit, both Meow Missile and Mouse Hunter need at least one 1 to trigger
  • Playing Nine Lives when health is merely low, save it for the moment you actually hit 0
  • Using Cat Nip on a mediocre first roll, doubling a bad roll is still a bad roll
  • Ignoring Feline Motor once active, track Tokyo's status during other players' turns
  • Using Mega Purr when opponents are resource-poor, it needs a full table to be worth play

monsters.name: Gigazaur
monsters.tagline: Raw nuclear power. Built to smash, not finesse.
monsters.content_html:
Overview

Gigazaur is a nuclear-powered wrecking machine with one goal: deal damage. His playstyle revolves around maximising claw output, keeping opponents on the back foot, and turning Tokyo into a warzone nobody else wants to enter.

Playstyle

Gigazaur is a Brawler. You win by eliminating monsters before they can build VP leads, or by hammering anyone who dares occupy Tokyo until they give it up. He rewards players who like making big, decisive swings — subtlety is not in his vocabulary.

Evolution Cards

Gigazaur's eight evolution cards amplify his already-aggressive dice game — turning raw rolls into precision tools and punishing anyone who dares hit back.

Tail Sweep (Permanent)

Once per turn, change one of your rolled dice to a 1 or 2.

A flexible utility card. Use it to complete a three-of-a-kind number combo you just missed, or fish for VP points when you're close to winning. Pairs well with Defender of Tokyo for squeezing extra value out of number dice.

Gamma Breath (Permanent)

Once per turn, change one of your rolled dice to a CLAW.

Gigazaur's bread and butter evolution. Guarantees at least one claw every turn, making your damage output completely reliable. Stack this with natural claw rolls for consistently high damage turns. One of his strongest permanents.

Heat Vision (Permanent)

Monsters that wound you with CLAWS lose 1 STAR.

A passive deterrent that punishes aggression against you. Opponents thinking about attacking will have to weigh losing VP — which can meaningfully slow down a Climber or Scorer. Best in a game where multiple players are attacking you.

Defender of Tokyo (Permanent)

If you start your turn in Tokyo, all other monsters lose 1 STAR.

Gigazaur's most powerful evolution. Turning Tokyo occupancy into a VP drain on every opponent per turn is devastating — it pressures Climbers and Scorers while rewarding you for staying put. Combine with Tail Sweep to keep rolling numbers and VP dice while this bleeds everyone dry.

Saurian Adaptability (Temporary)

Choose a die face. Take all dice showing that face and flip them all to a single face of your choice.

An incredible tempo card. If you rolled four lightning bolts but want claws, flip them all at once. Best used to convert a bad roll into a focused, high-value turn — especially when you need a burst of claws or a big three-of-a-kind.

Primal Bellow (Temporary)

All other monsters lose 2 STAR.

Two free VP off every opponent at once. In a four-player game that's 6 total VP swung in your favour in a single card. Save it for when multiple opponents are close to 20 VP, or use it as a closer when you're leading and want to stretch the gap.

Radioactive Waste (Temporary)

Gain 2 ENERGY and 1 HEART.

Gigazaur doesn't usually care about energy, but this freebie lets you pick up a card you'd otherwise ignore — something like Nova Breath or Extra Head that amplifies your damage further. The bonus heart is a small but welcome top-up.

Detachable Tail (Temporary)

You cannot lose HEART this turn.

A get-out-of-jail card. Use it on a turn where you're staying in Tokyo and expect heavy incoming damage, or hold it as insurance when you're low on health and can't afford to yield. Pairs beautifully with Defender of Tokyo — stay in Tokyo, drain opponents' VP, take zero damage.

Tips & Strategies

Early Game

  • Roll as many claws as possible in the first two rounds — establish board presence immediately.
  • Don't bother healing unless you're critically low; health is a resource to spend, not hoard.
  • Getting Gamma Breath early essentially gives you a free claw every turn — prioritise it.

Mid Game

  • Target the monster with the most VP, not the lowest health. VP leaders are the real threat.
  • Use your damage output to force expensive heals out of opponents, draining their reroll dice.
  • Once Defender of Tokyo is active, commit to staying in Tokyo as long as possible — the passive VP drain is worth absorbing hits.

Late Game

  • If you can eliminate a monster, commit to it fully — partial damage just lets them heal.
  • Switch to VP dice (3-of-a-kind) if you're already ahead; don't attack when you're winning.
  • Primal Bellow and Detachable Tail are ideal closers — save them for the final push.

Best Evolution Combos

  • Gamma Breath + Tail Sweep — guaranteed claw plus a flexible number conversion every turn. Consistent, dominant dice control.
  • Defender of Tokyo + Detachable Tail — stay in Tokyo for free, drain everyone's VP, take zero damage that turn.
  • Saurian Adaptability + Defender of Tokyo — convert a bad roll into a full VP dice turn while your Tokyo passive bleeds opponents.

Card Priorities (Base Market)

  1. Nova Breath — hits everyone outside Tokyo, synergises perfectly with your aggression
  2. Extra Head — doubles your claw output
  3. Complete Destruction — nuke the board when it counts

Common Mistakes

  • Spreading damage across multiple targets instead of focusing one down
  • Ignoring your own VP entirely and letting a sneaky camper hit 20
  • Over-committing to fights when a VP race is faster
  • Sitting on Detachable Tail too long — use it proactively when you need to stay in Tokyo, don't save it forever
  • Underestimating Defender of Tokyo — players often treat Gigazaur as a pure damage dealer and miss how powerful the VP drain is

monsters.name: Meka Dragon
monsters.content_html:
Overview

Meka Dragon is the card-game player's monster. While others are rolling dice and punching things, she's accumulating energy and converting it into powerful combos that snowball out of control. She has a lower floor than the tanks but a much higher ceiling in experienced hands — and her evolution cards turn her into something genuinely terrifying once they stack up.

Playstyle

Meka Dragon is an Engine Builder. Your dice rolls should almost always prioritise lightning bolts over everything else in the first few rounds. Once you have 5–8 energy, you can buy powerful cards that let you outpace everyone else's raw dice rolls.

This playstyle requires reading the card market carefully and being patient early. Her evolutions reward aggression in a very specific way — she doesn't just deal damage, she punishes monsters for daring to hit back.

Evolution Cards

Meka Dragon's eight evolution cards create a web of damage amplification and retribution. The more opponents attack her, the more dangerous she becomes.

Target Acquired (Permanent)

When a monster makes you lose HEART with CLAWS, you can give them the TARGET token. That monster loses 1 extra HEART every time you deal damage to them.

A slow-burn punisher. Mark whoever hits you hardest and every subsequent attack you land on them deals bonus damage. Pairs exceptionally well with Claws of Steel and Mecha Blast — the TARGET token turns any damage you deal into something much more threatening.

Claws of Steel (Permanent)

On your turn, if you make another monster lose at least 3 HEART, they lose 1 extra HEART.

A passive damage amplifier that rewards going big. Rolling three or more claws already puts opponents under pressure — this makes every strong claw turn deal 4+ damage instead of 3. Stack it with Target Acquired for a monster that punishes anyone you've marked.

Lightning Armor (Permanent)

When you lose HEART, roll a die for each HEART lost. For each CLAW rolled, the monster whose turn it is loses 1 HEART.

Meka Dragon's most defensive evolution — and one of the most disruptive in the game. Opponents attacking you have to gamble that their claw dice won't bounce straight back at them. In Tokyo, where you're absorbing multiple hits, this can reflect serious damage back across the table.

Breath of Doom (Permanent)

When you make monsters in Tokyo lose at least 1 HEART, monsters outside Tokyo also lose 1 HEART (except you).

A board-wide pressure tool. Hitting Tokyo now pings every monster not in Tokyo as collateral damage. Combined with Claws of Steel this can mean a single claw turn damages every monster on the board simultaneously. Devastating in a four-player game.

Tune-Up (Temporary)

Play before rolling dice. If you are not in Tokyo, skip your turn, gain 4 HEART and 2 ENERGY.

A deliberate trade — forfeit your turn entirely to fully restock. Best used when your health is dangerously low and your energy engine has stalled. The 2 energy ensures you can still buy something meaningful next turn. Never use it when you're in Tokyo — you need to be outside for it to trigger.

Programmed to Destroy (Permanent)

Gain 3 STAR and 2 ENERGY each time a monster's health reaches 0.

Meka Dragon's win condition accelerator. Each elimination hands her 3 VP and 2 energy — enough to buy another card or fuel a follow-up turn. In a game where you're already pressuring opponents with Target Acquired and Claws of Steel, this turns kills into a snowballing resource engine.

Destructive Analysis (Temporary)

Gain 1 ENERGY for each CLAW you rolled this turn.

The bridge between aggression and her energy engine. A turn where you rolled four claws suddenly also generates four energy — enough to buy a significant card immediately. Best used on a high-claw turn where you've already dealt your damage and want to convert the momentum into resources.

Mecha Blast (Temporary)

Each monster you make lose HEART with your CLAWS loses 2 extra HEART.

A massive burst damage card. Every monster you hit this turn takes 2 bonus damage — stack it with Target Acquired's bonus and Claws of Steel's threshold for a single turn that can cripple multiple monsters simultaneously. Save it for a decisive swing when opponents are already weakened.

Tips & Strategies

Early Game

  • Roll lightning bolts almost exclusively for the first 2–3 turns to build your engine.
  • Only enter Tokyo if it's handed to you — getting hit early disrupts your setup.
  • Getting Target Acquired early sets a threatening tone; mark the most aggressive player immediately.

Mid Game

  • By turn 4–5 you should have cards and evolutions stacking. Start chaining them.
  • Lightning Armor turns you into a dangerous target to attack — opponents will think twice.
  • Once Breath of Doom is active, every Tokyo hit becomes board-wide — prioritise claw dice when you have it.

Late Game

  • A fully built Meka Dragon can win in a single round with the right combo.
  • Programmed to Destroy makes finishing off weakened monsters a priority — each kill pays dividends.
  • Hold Mecha Blast for a turn where multiple opponents are low — the 2 bonus damage per target is devastating.

Best Evolution Combos

  • Target Acquired + Claws of Steel + Mecha Blast — mark a target, guarantee the threshold bonus, then play Mecha Blast for a turn that deals 3+ bonus damage to your marked target alone.
  • Breath of Doom + Claws of Steel — hit Tokyo hard enough to trigger the threshold, and every monster outside Tokyo takes 1 damage too. A single turn pings the whole board.
  • Lightning Armor + Programmed to Destroy — sit in Tokyo, reflect damage back, finish off whoever gets low enough and collect 3 VP and 2 energy per kill.
  • Destructive Analysis + any high-claw turn — convert aggression directly into energy, then immediately spend it on a card. Keeps both engines running simultaneously.

Card Priorities (Base Market)

  1. Apartment Building — massive VP burst, pairs with your energy advantage
  2. Nova Breath — hits all outside-Tokyo monsters, stacks with Breath of Doom
  3. Acid Attack — persistent damage that compounds over time
  4. Eater of the Dead — win condition in attrition games, combos with Programmed to Destroy

Common Mistakes

  • Buying expensive cards before your energy engine is established
  • Entering Tokyo too early — getting hit in setup turns stalls your engine before it starts
  • Forgetting to actually win — energy and evolutions are a means, not an end
  • Wasting Mecha Blast on a single target — it shines when you can hit multiple monsters at once
  • Not marking a target with Target Acquired early enough — every hit you take without marking someone is a missed opportunity
  • Using Tune-Up while in Tokyo — it only works outside, and staying in Tokyo while low on health is often the wrong call anyway

monsters.name: Space Penguin
monsters.tagline: The Antarctic Anarchist. Steals your tools, freezes your plans.
monsters.content_html:
Overview

Space Penguin is the most disruptive monster in the game. He doesn't win by dealing the most damage or sitting in Tokyo the longest — he wins by dismantling everyone else's strategy while quietly building his own. He copies evolution cards, locks dice, strips rerolls, and can freeze the entire board mid-game. In the right hands he's infuriating to play against and deeply satisfying to pilot.

He's rated Advanced because his value comes almost entirely from reading what opponents are doing and reacting to it. If you don't know what other monsters' evolutions do, Space Penguin loses most of his teeth.

Playstyle

Space Penguin is a Disruptor. He doesn't have a single fixed gameplan — his strategy reshapes itself around whatever the table is doing. His evolutions punish aggression, slow down fast decks, copy the best tools on the board, and deny opponents the rerolls they depend on.

His core loop is: observe what's working at the table → copy or counter it → buy time while opponents stall → close out with VP.

He rewards players who pay close attention to other monsters' boards and know when to disrupt versus when to accelerate their own game.

Evolution Cards

Space Penguin's eight evolution cards are among the most unique in the game — several of them interact directly with other players' cards and dice in ways no other monster can.

Icy Reflection (Permanent)

Choose an evolution card in front of another monster and place a Snowflake token on it. Icy Reflection becomes a copy of that card. If the copied card is removed from play, Icy Reflection is discarded.

Space Penguin's most defining card. You don't build your own engine — you steal someone else's. Copy Gigazaur's Gamma Breath for free claw manipulation, grab Meka Dragon's Lightning Armor to reflect damage, or clone The King's Twas Beauty Killed the Beast for instant VP generation. The card is only as strong as what's available to copy, which is why knowing other monsters' evolutions is essential. Watch out for the linked discard — if the original card leaves play, yours goes with it.

Black Diamond (Permanent)

Gain 1 extra STAR each time you take control of Tokyo or choose to stay in Tokyo when you could have yielded.

A VP engine that rewards decisiveness. Every time you actively choose to hold Tokyo under fire you bank a bonus star on top of the standard reward. It doesn't require staying long — even a series of quick in-and-out Tokyo grabs generates consistent VP. Pairs well with Cold Wave and Blizzard since limiting opponent dice makes staying in Tokyo much safer.

Freeze Ray (Permanent)

When you wound a monster in Tokyo, give them this card. At the start of their turn they choose a die face — that face has no effect this turn. You take the card back at the end of their turn.

A recurring debuff that bounces between you and Tokyo's occupant. Each time you hit the Tokyo monster, they lose a die face for their next turn — no claws, no lightning bolts, no hearts, whatever they most need gone. It doesn't cost you anything to keep triggering it as long as you keep dealing damage. Particularly strong against energy-dependent monsters who rely on lightning bolt turns.

Encased in Ice (Permanent)

Spend 1 ENERGY to choose one of your rolled dice. That die is frozen until the start of your next turn — it cannot be changed and is used normally by other monsters during the Resolve Dice phase.

Dice manipulation with a twist. Lock in a claw, a lightning bolt, or a number die before rerolling the rest — guaranteeing at least one result. The frozen die also interacts with other players' Resolve Dice effects, which can be used tactically to interfere with cards that trigger off specific faces. Costs energy but pays off consistently as a precision tool.

Miraculous Catch (Permanent)

Once per turn during the Buy Power Cards phase, shuffle the discard pile and reveal one card randomly. Buy it for 1 ENERGY less than normal or discard it. The rest of the discard pile goes back.

Access to cards everyone else already passed on — at a discount. The randomness is real but the energy reduction means you can snap up expensive cards that would otherwise be out of reach. Best used when the face-up market is weak or when you know a powerful card was recently discarded. Stacks well with Space Penguin's energy generation to keep buying every turn.

Deep Dive (Temporary)

Look at the top 3 Power cards of the deck. Choose one and play it in front of you for free. Put the others on the bottom.

A free card from the top of the deck with full information. Unlike Miraculous Catch this is deterministic — you see three options and pick the best one. Playing a keep card for free is always strong. Use it when the face-up market has nothing you want or when you need a specific type of card to complete a combo.

Cold Wave (Temporary)

Until your next turn, all other monsters roll with 1 fewer die.

Board-wide disruption on a timer. Every opponent loses a die for a full round — fewer claws, fewer lightning bolts, fewer VP numbers. Best played on your turn just before a round where multiple opponents are likely to take aggressive or high-value turns. Combine with Blizzard in the same round for total dice lockdown.

Blizzard (Temporary)

Play during your turn. Until the start of your next turn, monsters only have a single roll and cannot yield Tokyo.

Space Penguin's most powerful disruption card. Two effects at once: strip all rerolls from every opponent for a full round, and lock the current Tokyo occupant in place — they cannot yield no matter how much damage they take. Use it when a monster in Tokyo is low on health to guarantee they take everything coming at them, or when multiple opponents are sitting on powerful dice combinations they were planning to reroll into.

Tips & Strategies

Early Game

  • Scout other monsters' evolutions immediately — Icy Reflection is your most valuable card and its ceiling depends entirely on what's available to copy.
  • Roll lightning bolts early. Your kit is energy-hungry and you need reserves for Encased in Ice and Miraculous Catch.
  • Freeze Ray is most effective against high-energy monsters early — crippling their lightning bolt turns before their engine starts is much easier than countering it mid-game.

Mid Game

  • Icy Reflection targets should shift as the game evolves — copy whatever is currently the strongest active evolution on the board.
  • Use Cold Wave or Blizzard when opponents are about to take a turn that could end the game or significantly change the board state.
  • Black Diamond rewards opportunistic Tokyo grabs — you don't need to camp, just dip in and out decisively.

Late Game

  • Blizzard is your best closer — locking rerolls and forcing Tokyo hold means a weak occupant can't escape punishment.
  • If you're near 20 VP, Deep Dive can find the card that gets you there immediately.
  • Watch for Miraculous Catch value late when the discard pile is full of expensive cards other players passed on.

Best Evolution Combos

  • Icy Reflection + any strong permanent — copying The King's Twas Beauty Killed the Beast or Gigazaur's Defender of Tokyo gives you VP generation you never had to earn.
  • Cold Wave + Blizzard — play both in the same round for a full dice lockdown. Opponents get one roll with one fewer die and no rerolls. Devastating during a critical round.
  • Freeze Ray + Encased in Ice — debuff the Tokyo occupant's dice while locking in your best result before rerolling. Controls both sides of the roll simultaneously.
  • Black Diamond + Blizzard — force the Tokyo occupant to stay put, then grab Tokyo yourself on a subsequent turn for the bonus VP.

Card Priorities (Base Market)

  1. Freeze Time — stalling opponents while your disruption compounds is extremely powerful
  2. Apartment Building — converts your energy advantage into VP efficiently
  3. Herd Culler — cheap damage that keeps opponents' health low for Blizzard finishes
  4. Background Dweller — staying out of Tokyo safely while accumulating energy suits Space Penguin's patient gameplan

Common Mistakes

  • Using Icy Reflection on a weak evolution just because it's available — wait for something genuinely powerful to copy
  • Playing Blizzard too early before multiple opponents are vulnerable — it's a one-shot, make it count
  • Ignoring energy generation — Encased in Ice and Miraculous Catch both cost energy and run dry fast without lightning bolt investment
  • Forgetting that Freeze Ray bounces back to you — don't lose track of it or you lose the recurring debuff
  • Treating Space Penguin like a damage dealer — his aggression stats are low for a reason, VP comes from disruption and card value, not claws
  • Using Cold Wave and Blizzard on separate turns when stacking them in the same round is dramatically more effective

monsters.name: The King
monsters.tagline: King of the concrete jungle. Tokyo is his throne — and he intends to keep it.
monsters.content_html:
Overview

The King is a giant gorilla with one obsession: owning Tokyo. Every evolution card he has either rewards him for being in Tokyo, punishes opponents for trying to remove him, or gets him back in the moment he's forced out. He's not interested in energy or card engines — he wants the throne, and he wants it now.

He's a strong pick for players who enjoy positional play and VP accumulation over raw aggression.

Playstyle

The King is a Defender. His entire kit is built around controlling Tokyo and converting that control into victory points faster than any other monster. Where other monsters treat Tokyo as a tactical tool, The King treats it as a permanent residence.

His core loop is: get into Tokyo → stay there → stack VP from multiple sources simultaneously → never let anyone take it.

The twist is that his evolutions also give him tools to reclaim Tokyo instantly if he's ever forced out — making him uniquely slippery for a monster whose whole identity is standing still.

Evolution Cards

The King's eight evolution cards are almost entirely focused on Tokyo control and VP generation. Getting even two or three of them active simultaneously creates a VP engine that's very hard to race against.

Twas Beauty Killed the Beast (Permanent)

While in Tokyo, gain 1 STAR at the end of every monster's turn including yours. Discard this card and lose all your STAR if you leave Tokyo.

The King's most powerful and most dangerous evolution. In a four-player game, this generates 4 VP per full round — compounding fast. The catch is brutal: leaving Tokyo costs you every star you've accumulated on this card. Never play it unless you're committed to staying put, have enough health to absorb pressure, and have Simian Scamper or Chest Thumping ready as insurance.

I Am the King! (Permanent)

Gain 1 extra STAR when you take control of Tokyo or start your turn there.

A straightforward VP amplifier that stacks with the base game's existing Tokyo rewards. Taking Tokyo now gives 2 VP instead of 1, and starting your turn there gives 2 VP instead of 1. Low risk, permanent benefit — get this out early and it pays off every single turn.

Alpha Male (Permanent)

If you wound at least one monster with CLAWS, gain 1 STAR.

Turns every claw turn into a VP gain regardless of whether you're in Tokyo. Makes The King a legitimate VP threat even on turns he's outside Tokyo — and means opponents can't ignore him just because he yielded. Stack with I Am the King! for a monster generating VP from multiple sources every turn.

Chest Thumping (Permanent)

You can force monsters you wound with CLAWS to yield Tokyo.

Control made absolute. Instead of waiting for an opponent to choose to yield, you decide. Hit a Tokyo occupant hard enough and you can eject them immediately and claim the throne — no negotiation. Pairs perfectly with Twas Beauty Killed the Beast, since getting back into Tokyo on demand protects your VP stack.

Giant Banana (Temporary)

Gain 2 HEART.

Simple sustain. Use it when your health drops low enough that staying in Tokyo becomes risky. Two health can be the difference between riding out one more round of attacks and being forced to yield and losing everything on Twas Beauty Killed the Beast.

Jungle Frenzy (Temporary)

Play at the end of your movement phase, if you wounded the Tokyo controller with CLAWS but didn't take control. Take an extra turn.

A precision card that rewards aggressive outside play. If you hit the Tokyo occupant hard enough to wound them but they didn't yield, you get a full bonus turn immediately. Use it to keep pressing — attack again, roll for VP, or set up the killing blow. One of the strongest tempo cards in The King's kit.

Simian Scamper (Temporary)

If you yield Tokyo, do not lose HEART. You can't lose HEART this turn.

The safety net for Twas Beauty Killed the Beast. If you're forced to yield and your accumulated stars would be lost, at least you walk away at full health. Also useful as a general defensive card when opponents are stacking up claw damage and you need to survive the round.

Monkey Rush (Temporary)

Play when the monster controlling Tokyo leaves or is eliminated. Take control of Tokyo.

An instant Tokyo grab triggered by someone else's misfortune. Hold this in hand and you're always one monster's bad luck away from reclaiming the throne. Devastatingly good when played in response to a high-health monster being forced out — you slide in before anyone else can react.

Tips & Strategies

Early Game

  • Get into Tokyo as fast as possible and establish I Am the King! first — it's low risk and pays off every turn from the moment it hits.
  • Hold Monkey Rush in hand at all times until you need Tokyo back urgently.
  • Roll claws early to pressure the current Tokyo occupant and set up Chest Thumping.

Mid Game

  • Once you're established in Tokyo, play Twas Beauty Killed the Beast — but only when you have enough health to commit.
  • Keep Giant Banana or Simian Scamper as insurance before activating Twas Beauty Killed the Beast.
  • Use Chest Thumping to control who occupies Tokyo — eject whoever poses the biggest threat.

Late Game

  • With Twas Beauty Killed the Beast and I Am the King! both active, you're generating 5+ VP per round passively. Play defensively and let the VP clock win for you.
  • Jungle Frenzy is a strong closer — a bonus turn when you're near 20 VP can end the game immediately.
  • Watch opponents' energy stockpiles carefully. A player who can one-shot your health and force a yield is your biggest threat.

Best Evolution Combos

  • Twas Beauty Killed the Beast + I Am the King! + Alpha Male — VP from three sources every round. Each turn in Tokyo generates 2 VP from I Am the King!, up to 4 VP from Twas Beauty, and 1 VP from any claw turn. Opponents must deal with you immediately or lose the VP race.
  • Chest Thumping + Monkey Rush — force anyone out of Tokyo with claws, then instantly reclaim it with Monkey Rush if you ever lose it. Near-permanent Tokyo control.
  • Twas Beauty Killed the Beast + Simian Scamper — stay in Tokyo accumulating VP safely, with Simian Scamper as the escape hatch that doesn't cost you health when you finally have to yield.

Card Priorities (Base Market)

  1. Regeneration — keeps you in Tokyo longer, protecting your VP stack
  2. Even Bigger — extra health extends your Tokyo stays significantly
  3. Armor Plating — reduces incoming damage, making you harder to force out
  4. Domination — if you're already winning the VP race, extra VP from Tokyo turns closes it faster

Common Mistakes

  • Activating Twas Beauty Killed the Beast too early before you have health insurance — losing all your accumulated stars to a forced yield is catastrophic
  • Holding Monkey Rush too long and letting someone else grab Tokyo when you needed it
  • Ignoring Alpha Male — VP from claw turns outside Tokyo is often overlooked and adds up faster than expected
  • Staying in Tokyo at 3-4 health hoping to survive one more round — know when to yield intentionally before being forced out
  • Forgetting that Chest Thumping is proactive, not reactive — use it to eject threats before they become dangerous, not after
